questions.question: In the classification used by practices such as ASTM E1417, which developer forms are the nonaqueous wet developers?
options: Forms a and b
Forms d and e — d for Type I, e for Type II
Forms b and c
Form f only
questions.explanation: Forms d and e are the solvent-carried nonaqueous wet developers — form d paired with fluorescent systems and form e with visible systems. They are the most sensitive forms in common spot use.

questions.question: Which penetrant–developer pairing do typical practices prohibit?
options: Fluorescent penetrant with dry powder developer
Visible penetrant with nonaqueous developer
Water-washable penetrant with water-soluble (form b) developer
Post-emulsifiable penetrant with water-suspendible developer
questions.explanation: A water-based soluble coating can keep dissolving and washing water-washable penetrant out of the flaws it is supposed to develop, so common practice keeps form b away from Method A systems (and from Type II).

questions.question: The right way to lay nonaqueous developer on a dried part is:
options: Shake the can until the mixing ball rattles freely, hold it roughly 8–12 inches away, and apply several light passes that each flash off
One slow, heavy pass close to the surface for full opacity
Apply it before the dryer so the solvent and water evaporate together
Pour a small amount onto the surface and spread it with a brush
questions.explanation: Nonaqueous developer works as a thin, even, fast-drying film on a completely dry surface. Several light passes give that film; a heavy coat buries fine indications.

questions.question: Visible (Type II) penetrant inspection requires which light condition at the examination surface?
options: UV-A of at least 1,000 µW/cm²
About 20 lux of white light
At least about 100 footcandles — roughly 1,000 lux — of white light
Total darkness, with a flashlight for spot checks
questions.explanation: Red-on-white contrast needs strong white light: typical procedure values call for at least 100 fc, about 1,000 lux, measured at the surface. The UV and darkness rules belong to fluorescent work.

questions.question: Which set of habits keeps a fluorescent inspection booth legitimate over time?
options: Replace the lamp monthly and skip the metering
Warm the lamp up, measure UV-A at the surface with a radiometer on schedule and after bulb changes, and verify booth ambient stays at or below about 2 fc
Measure UV at the lamp face once a year
Rely on dark adaptation to compensate for a weakening lamp
questions.explanation: The numbers that matter are at the inspection surface, and they drift — lamps age and filters degrade. Periodic measured checks of UV intensity and ambient light keep the booth valid.

questions.question: A broken line of aligned bleed-out segments appears along a machined surface and returns after a careful solvent-damp wipe and redevelopment. The best first-look call is:
options: False indication from lint
Nonrelevant indication from part geometry
Rounded porosity
A partially closed linear discontinuity — such as a crack or lap ground over at the surface
questions.explanation: Intermittent linear indications point to flaws partly closed at the surface, often by machining or grinding. Re-bleed after a wipe confirms penetrant feeding from real volume — not lint or geometry.

questions.question: Aqueous developer film is left on carbon-steel parts over a humid weekend. The most likely result is:
options: Corrosion staining and pitting under the residue, because the film holds moisture against the steel
The film hardens into a useful protective coating
Indications continue developing and improve for Monday's re-read
No effect — aqueous developers act as corrosion inhibitors
questions.explanation: Developer residues are hygroscopic: they hold humidity against the surface and set up corrosion cells. Postcleaning happens promptly after documentation for exactly this reason.

questions.question: For removing dry powder developer residue from a finished part, the right first choice is:
options: Wire brushing until bare metal shows
A light abrasive blast
Low-pressure air blow-off or a plain water rinse — the mildest method that works
Soaking the part in strong acid
questions.explanation: Postcleaning uses the mildest effective method. Brushing and blasting smear metal over flaw openings and compromise any future penetrant test; acids leave residues that can kill fluorescence.

questions.question: Water-washable fluorescent process with dry powder developer — the correct order is:
options: Pre-clean → penetrant → developer → rinse → dry → inspect → postclean
Pre-clean and dry → penetrant dwell → rinse → dry → dry powder developer → development dwell → inspect → postclean
Pre-clean → rinse → penetrant dwell → dry powder developer → dry → inspect → postclean
Penetrant dwell → pre-clean → rinse → developer → inspect → postclean
questions.explanation: Dry powder must go onto a dry surface, so the part is dried after the rinse and before the developer — then development dwell, the read, and postcleaning close the line.

questions.question: A part arrives at 30 °F from outdoor storage for a standard penetrant test whose band is the typical 40–125 °F. What happens?
options: Test it normally — cold parts hold penetrant better
Double the developer time to compensate
Rinse with hot water so the part warms during removal
Bring the part into the temperature band — or use a procedure qualified for low temperature — before penetrant goes on
questions.explanation: Below the standard band, penetrant thickens and capillary entry slows badly. The part is warmed into the band first, unless a specially qualified low-temperature procedure applies. Downstream tweaks do not fix a cold application.
